name: Norway
summary: An oil-wealthy Nordic state with very high tech salaries, the world's 2nd-best rule of law (WJP), a top passport, universal healthcare and dual citizenship retained both ways (Norway allows it since 2020 and sits within Bangladesh's 'all of Europe' DNC scope) — but constrained for this profile by an 8-year/B1-Norwegian citizenship path, newly introduced non-EEA tuition (from 2023), extreme living costs, a small Oslo-centric market, a domestic halal-slaughter ban and a small Bangladeshi community. Derived overall sits just below the 60% inclusion line.

job-market.summary: Very high tech salaries in a growing Oslo market, but the market is small and Norwegian is often expected.

pros.text: Very high pay: software engineers in Oslo earn ~NOK 760k–1.2m and AI engineers ~$126k average, with top AI/cloud packages reaching NOK 1.5–2.1m.

cons.text: The market is small and Oslo-centric, and Norwegian is commonly expected outside international firms.

visa-access.summary: No Norwegian mission in Dhaka and a high funds requirement (NOK 325,400/yr), though no Bangladesh-specific ban.

pros.text: No Bangladesh-specific visa ban; the UDI process is transparent.

cons.text: Norway has no mission in Bangladesh (processed via a third country), and the funds requirement is high (NOK ~325,400/yr).

citizenship.summary: Dual citizenship is retained both ways and the passport is elite (Henley 4th), but naturalisation requires 8 years plus a B1 Norwegian exam (raised from A2) and a citizenship test.

pros.text: Norway has allowed dual citizenship since 2020 (no renunciation), and as a European country it falls within Bangladesh's 'all of Europe' DNC scope, so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality.
The Norwegian passport ranks joint 4th on Henley 2026 (~185 destinations).

cons.text: Naturalisation requires 8 years' residence (of the last 11) plus a B1 Norwegian oral exam (raised from A2) and a citizenship test — a long, language-heavy path.

post-study-work.summary: A 12-month job-seeker permit (any job, non-extendable) converts deterministically to a quota-free skilled-worker permit on a qualifying offer; each spouse qualifies.

pros.text: Graduates can stay 12 months on a job-seeker permit, working any job during the search, then convert to a skilled-worker permit; each spouse qualifies.

cons.text: The job-seeker permit is one-time and non-extendable (12 months), and requires NOK ~325,400 of funds.

spouse-family.summary: Family of skilled workers get residence and work rights with free schooling; if both spouses study, each holds an independent track.

pros.text: Family members of a skilled worker get residence with work rights, children attend school free, and if both spouses study/work each holds their own permit.

cons.text: Family immigration carries income requirements, demanding given Norway's living costs.

msc-study.summary: Good English-taught CS/AI at NTNU and Oslo, but Norway began charging non-EEA tuition in 2023 (previously free), and living costs are extreme.

pros.text: NTNU and the University of Oslo offer strong English-taught CS/AI master's; a master qualifies for the job-seeker permit.

cons.text: Norway introduced tuition fees for non-EEA students from autumn 2023 (previously free), and the cost of living is among the world's highest.

pr-pathway.summary: Permanent residence is reachable after ~3 years of skilled work — fast by EU standards — though it requires Norwegian-language/integration completion.

pros.text: Permanent residence is reachable after ~3 years of continuous skilled-work residence, faster than many EU peers.

cons.text: PR requires completing Norwegian-language and social-studies/integration tuition, a real burden for English-first applicants.

income-cost.summary: Very high salaries leave a strong surplus despite extreme costs, but the study phase (now with tuition) and high housing bite.

pros.text: Very high tech salaries support strong savings and remittance even at Norwegian price levels.

cons.text: Living costs are among the world's highest, and the study phase now carries tuition plus a high funds requirement.

links.title: Study in Norway — cost of living (official)

healthcare.summary: Universal national-insurance healthcare for residents/workers with strong oil-funded welfare and excellent subsidised childcare.

pros.text: Residents and workers (and students staying 12+ months) are covered by the National Insurance Scheme, with an oil-funded welfare net and excellent subsidised childcare.

cons.text: Some treatment waiting lists exist, and full coverage depends on registration/length of stay.

culture-language.summary: Norwegians' English is excellent and livability is world-leading, but Norwegian is required for status (B1) and a real daily-life barrier outside work.

pros.text: Norwegians have excellent English (Oslo work and daily life largely workable in English), with world-leading livability and work-life balance, and an established South-Asian (Pakistani) community.

cons.text: Norwegian is required for PR and citizenship (B1) and for full daily integration outside international workplaces.

safety-law.summary: Among the safest countries with the world's 2nd-best rule of law (WJP), tempered by the legacy of the 2011 attack and a domestic halal-slaughter ban.

pros.text: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 2nd worldwide; very low crime, strong women's safety and reliable, low-corruption institutions.

cons.text: The 2011 Utøya/Oslo far-right attack and subsequent anti-Muslim incidents remain reference points, and Norway bans domestic halal (and kosher) slaughter on animal-welfare grounds.

links.title: World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index 2025

politics.summary: A stable democracy where the right-populist Progress Party is a significant force pushing migration restriction, though skilled migration stays open.

pros.text: Institutions are very stable, and skilled-worker migration channels remain open and economically valued.

cons.text: The right-populist Progress Party (FrP) is a major force pushing migration restriction, and the halal-slaughter ban signals limited religious accommodation.

tax.summary: A moderate-high wedge (marginal ~47%) on very high salaries, with no expat regime but a Norway–Bangladesh treaty.

pros.text: High gross salaries cushion the burden, and a Norway–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty supports repatriation.

cons.text: The marginal tax/social wedge is ~47% with no special expat-relief regime.

community-belonging.summary: A ~3–6% Muslim population with ~50 mosques (two-thirds in Oslo) and a large Pakistani community for South-Asian ecosystem, but imported-only halal (domestic slaughter banned) and a small Bangladeshi community.

pros.text: Muslims are ~3–6% with around 50 mosques (two-thirds in the Oslo area), and a large Pakistani-Norwegian community (~39,000) provides a strong South-Asian ecosystem; imported halal food is available.

cons.text: Domestic halal slaughter is banned (halal meat is imported), the Bangladeshi-specific community is small, and the FrP-influenced climate is cool toward Muslim migration.

direct-work-route.summary: A strong, clean route that Bangladeshis underuse. The skilled-worker residence permit is quota-free with no labour-market test: a BSc software engineer with a full-time offer at/above the pay floor (NOK 522,600 bachelor / 599,200 master from Sept 2025, easily cleared by Oslo tech pay) qualifies, and skilled workers can request early-employment-start from the police. PR comes unusually fast — 3 years (+ A2 Norwegian + social-studies test) — and citizenship at ~7 years (B1 oral), with dual citizenship allowed since 2020 (Norway within Bangladesh's all-Europe DNC scope). The real frictions are practical, not legal: no Norwegian mission in Dhaka (apply via a third country) and documented name-based hiring bias. Note: Bangladesh's UDI 'heightened scrutiny' flag applies to vocational trades, not university-degree software roles.

pros.text: Quota-free skilled-worker permit, no labour-market test, early-employment-start option, and PR in just 3 years → citizenship ~7 years; dual citizenship retained (since 2020).
Acute tech shortage (~16,000 openings 2025) with a light employer burden (no sponsor licence/levy).

cons.text: No Norwegian visa mission in Dhaka — biometrics/submission via a third-country VFS adds cost and time; documented name-based hiring callback bias.
Small BD community (~3,080 background) and thin BD-tech track record; A2 Norwegian for PR and B1 oral for citizenship.
